justification: All pass criteria met: (1) 4 bounded contexts identified — Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification. (2) Cross-context dependencies explicitly called out — spec notes 'The spec conflates these into one feature description, which is a design risk' and edge cases address cross-context issues like recalculation triggers from delivery lifecycle events. (3) Vehicle class constraint correctly marked invariant:true (BR-6), stop duration correctly marked invariant:false (BR-7/BR-8), notification window correctly marked invariant:false (BR-5). (4) 10 acceptance criteria provided, well above the 4 minimum. (5) Route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated: new delivery added (BR-2), delivery marked failed (BR-3), driver manual deviation (BR-4).

observations.text: Identified 4 distinct bounded contexts with clear naming and explicit warning about the spec conflating them
10 well-structured Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering core flows and edge cases like notification window transitions
Excellent edge case coverage — 10 scenarios including concurrent driver assignment, notification oscillation, and shift-end handoff
Vehicle class constraint (BR-6) and zone constraint (BR-9) correctly classified as invariant:true with strong justification
Stop duration default (BR-7) and notification window (BR-5) correctly classified as invariant:false

Recalculation triggers (BR-2, BR-3, BR-4) marked invariant:false — the rubric expected invariant:true since the system must always recalculate on these events. Bird's justification that the policy could change is reasonable but disagrees with the expected classification.
Cross-context dependency is mentioned in bounded_context description but not formally surfaced as a structured integration point for MJ — no explicit escalation or integration_points section
Rich ubiquitous language section with 11 well-defined domain terms
Business impact analysis is thorough with stakeholder-level breakdown
